project.name: ReasonGraph MVP Examples
project.description: Seed examples for testing business reasoning and disagreement structuring.

issue.title: Vendor selection
issue.business_context: The company must decide whether to buy a vendor solution or build the capability internally.
issue.decision_question: Should the company buy Vendor X, build internally, or use a hybrid approach?
issue.in_scope: Time to value, total cost of ownership, vendor lock-in, internal capability, data portability, integration complexity, strategic control.
issue.out_of_scope: General opinions about outsourcing, unrelated technology stack debates.

positions.label: Buy Vendor X
positions.recommendation: Buy Vendor X because the company needs the capability quickly.
positions.reasoning_text: Vendor X already provides most of the required functionality. Buying reduces implementation time and allows operations to improve processes sooner.
positions.claims_text: Vendor X can be deployed faster than an internal build. The current process pain is urgent. Delaying the capability has operational costs.
positions.assumptions_text: Vendor X can integrate with current systems. Vendor implementation timelines are realistic. The vendor can meet support expectations.
positions.evidence_text: Vendor demos show coverage of core workflows. Reference customers report faster deployment than internal builds.
positions.criteria_text: Time to value, operational reliability, support quality, implementation risk.
positions.risks_text: Vendor lock-in, contract inflexibility, integration gaps.
positions.would_change_mind: This position would weaken if technical discovery shows major integration blockers or hidden implementation costs.

positions.label: Build internally
positions.recommendation: Build the capability internally to preserve control and avoid long-term vendor dependency.
positions.reasoning_text: The capability is strategically important. Building internally allows the company to customize workflows, control architecture, and avoid vendor lock-in.
positions.claims_text: Internal build provides more control. Vendor lock-in could become costly. The company already has some technical capability required for the build.
positions.assumptions_text: Engineering has enough capacity. The requirements will evolve in ways that require customization. Long-term control is more valuable than short-term speed.
positions.evidence_text: Existing internal systems already cover part of the workflow. Engineering has built similar internal tools before.
positions.criteria_text: Strategic control, flexibility, long-term cost, architecture fit, data ownership.
positions.risks_text: Delivery delay, engineering capacity constraints, unclear requirements, maintenance burden.
positions.would_change_mind: This position would weaken if the capability is not strategic or if the vendor contract includes strong portability and customization guarantees.

positions.label: Hybrid vendor-first approach
positions.recommendation: Start with Vendor X but negotiate exit clauses, data portability, and integration safeguards.
positions.reasoning_text: A vendor-first approach can deliver value quickly while reducing long-term lock-in risk through contract design and architecture safeguards.
positions.claims_text: Contract terms can reduce vendor dependency. A phased approach lowers immediate delivery risk. Data portability is essential if the company later builds internally.
positions.assumptions_text: Vendor X will accept reasonable contract safeguards. The company can design integrations to avoid excessive dependency. A later transition remains possible.
positions.evidence_text: Procurement can include exit clauses, service-level agreements, and data export requirements in the contract.
positions.criteria_text: Time to value, contractual flexibility, risk reduction, data portability, implementation control.
positions.risks_text: Vendor refuses key terms, hybrid approach creates complexity, future migration is harder than expected.
positions.would_change_mind: This position would weaken if Vendor X refuses data portability or if internal build estimates are faster than expected.
